page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.r6.large is a memory-optimized instance built on the KVM hypervisor. It features 2 dedicated vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor running at 2.5 GHz. The system is equipped with 16.0 GB of RAM, providing a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, and it provides a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps.

Benchmark results indicate modest processing and memory throughput capabilities. The single-core stress-ng performance score is 1298.79, while the multi-core score is 1726.78. Memory bandwidth tests show 96.46 GB/sec for small cached blocks, dropping to 13.08 GB/sec for large uncached reads and 11.37 GB/sec for large uncached writes. Database operations under Passmark scored 675.44, and Redis SET operations reached 1.67 million operations per second.

Given its dedicated but lower-tier CPU performance and high memory density, this instance offers a cost-efficient entry point for memory-bound workloads that do not require intensive computational power. It is suitable for small-scale caching layers, lightweight database hosting, and memory-intensive background utilities where high CPU throughput is not a primary requirement.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.r6.large is a memory-optimized instance featuring 2 dedicated vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor clocked at 2.5 GHz. It is configured with 16.0 GB of RAM, yielding a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core, though it lacks local storage and GPU accelerators. Operating on the KVM hypervisor, the instance delivers a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps. Performance benchmarks place this server in the lower tiers, with a stress-ng single-core score of 1298.79 and a multi-core score of 1726.78. Memory bandwidth is measured at 13.08 GB/sec for large uncached reads. This hardware profile is best suited for memory-intensive but computationally light workloads, such as small-scale caching, Redis deployments, and lightweight database operations where memory capacity is prioritized over raw processing speed.
